title: Temperature & Sampling
eli8:
# The Creativity Thermostat 🌡️

Imagine the AI is picking the next word from a bag of options. **Temperature** controls how it picks!

## Low Temperature = Careful Picker 🎯

At **temperature = 0**, the AI is like a very careful student who always picks the most common, safest answer:
- "The sky is..." → Always picks **"blue"**
- Great for math problems and coding!
- Boring but correct ✓

## Medium Temperature = Natural Talker 💬

At **temperature = 0.7**, the AI is like a normal person having a conversation:
- "The sky is..." → Might say **"blue"** (60%), **"clear"** (25%), **"beautiful"** (10%), or something else (5%)
- Sounds natural and varied!
- Great for chatting and explaining things 😊

## High Temperature = Creative Artist 🎨

At **temperature = 1.5** or **2.0**, the AI is like an artist who loves surprises:
- "The sky is..." → Might say **"weeping"**, **"a canvas of forgotten dreams"**, or even **"hungry"**!
- Wild and creative, but can make mistakes
- Great for brainstorming and poetry 🎭

## Other Tricks: Top-K and Top-P 🎰

The AI doesn't just have temperature — it has other knobs too:

- **Top-K = 50**: "Only choose from the 50 most likely next words!"
  - Like telling the AI: "No weird words allowed!"

- **Top-P = 0.9**: "Keep picking likely words until you've covered 90% of the probability!"
  - Smarter than Top-K — automatically adjusts to how sure the AI is

These controls let you tune the AI like a music mixer! 🎛️
detail:
# Temperature & Sampling

## From Logits to Probabilities

At each generation step, the LLM outputs a vector of **logits** — one raw score per vocabulary token. These are converted to a probability distribution via **softmax**:

```python
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F

logits = torch.tensor([2.0, 1.5, 0.5, -1.0, 3.0])  # 5 hypothetical tokens
probs = F.softmax(logits, dim=-1)
# → [0.197, 0.119, 0.044, 0.008, 0.537] (sums to 1.0)
```

## Temperature Scaling

Before applying softmax, logits are divided by temperature T:

```python
def temperature_sample(logits, temperature=1.0):
    if temperature == 0:
        return logits.argmax()  # greedy decoding
    scaled_logits = logits / temperature
    probs = F.softmax(scaled_logits, dim=-1)
    return torch.multinomial(probs, num_samples=1)
```

**Effect of temperature on distributions:**

| Temperature | Effect | Use Case |
|------------|--------|----------|
| 0.0 | Greedy — always picks max | Code, math, factual Q&A |
| 0.2–0.5 | Conservative — peaked distribution | Technical writing, structured output |
| 0.7–1.0 | Balanced — natural variation | General chat, explanations |
| 1.2–1.5 | Creative — flatter distribution | Brainstorming, poetry |
| 2.0+ | Chaotic — near-uniform | Experimental only |

T < 1 sharpens the distribution (more peaked), T > 1 flattens it (more uniform), T = 1 leaves it unchanged.

## Greedy Decoding

Always select the highest-probability token. Deterministic, reproducible, but:
- Produces repetitive, formulaic text
- Can get stuck in degenerate loops
- Often not the highest-quality output despite seeming "confident"

## Top-K Sampling

Before sampling, zero out all but the K highest-probability tokens, then renormalize:

```python
def top_k_sampling(logits, k=50, temperature=1.0):
    # Zero out all but top-k
    top_k_logits, top_k_indices = torch.topk(logits, k)
    filtered = torch.full_like(logits, float('-inf'))
    filtered.scatter_(0, top_k_indices, top_k_logits)
    scaled = filtered / temperature
    probs = F.softmax(scaled, dim=-1)
    return torch.multinomial(probs, num_samples=1)
```

**Limitation**: K is fixed regardless of the distribution shape. When the model is very confident (one token has 90% probability), K=50 still forces competition with 49 unlikely options.

## Top-P (Nucleus) Sampling

Sample from the smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability exceeds P:

```python
def top_p_sampling(logits, p=0.9, temperature=1.0):
    sorted_logits, sorted_indices = torch.sort(logits, descending=True)
    probs = F.softmax(sorted_logits / temperature, dim=-1)
    cumulative_probs = torch.cumsum(probs, dim=-1)
    # Remove tokens with cumulative prob > p (shift right to include the crossing token)
    sorted_indices_to_remove = cumulative_probs - probs > p
    sorted_logits[sorted_indices_to_remove] = float('-inf')
    # Scatter back to original order
    logits_filtered = sorted_logits.scatter(0, sorted_indices, sorted_logits)
    return torch.multinomial(F.softmax(logits_filtered / temperature, dim=-1), 1)
```

Top-P adapts to distribution shape: when the model is certain, the nucleus may contain only 2–3 tokens; when uncertain, it may include 50+ tokens.

## Min-P Sampling

A more recent alternative (Nguyen et al., 2023): filter out tokens whose probability falls below `min_p × p_max` (where p_max is the highest token probability). Scales the threshold relative to the most likely token, performing well at high temperatures.

## Beam Search

Instead of sampling one token at a time, maintain the B most probable sequences (beams) at each step. Guarantees higher probability sequences but:
- Deterministic (no randomness)
- Computationally expensive (B× more compute)
- Often produces generic, repetitive text for open-ended generation
- Still used in machine translation and structured prediction

## Repetition Penalty

A technique to discourage the model from repeating tokens it has already generated:
```python
# Divide logits of previously seen tokens by a penalty factor
for token_id in past_token_ids:
    logits[token_id] /= repetition_penalty  # e.g., 1.1 to 1.3
```

## Practical Settings

| Task | Temperature | Top-P | Notes |
|------|-------------|-------|-------|
| Code generation | 0.0–0.2 | 0.95 | Correctness over creativity |
| Factual Q&A | 0.0–0.3 | 0.9 | Deterministic answers |
| Chat/conversation | 0.7 | 0.9 | Natural variation |
| Creative writing | 0.9–1.1 | 0.95 | Varied but coherent |
| Poetry/brainstorm | 1.0–1.5 | 1.0 | Maximize diversity |

mcqs.question: What happens to the probability distribution when you set temperature to a value less than 1?
options: The distribution becomes flatter, giving more probability to unlikely tokens
The distribution becomes more peaked, concentrating probability on the most likely tokens
All tokens receive equal probability
The distribution is unchanged; only sampling speed is affected
mcqs.explanation: Dividing logits by T < 1 amplifies differences between them, making the softmax distribution more peaked. The most likely token gets even more probability, and unlikely tokens get less. T > 1 does the opposite — flattening the distribution.

mcqs.question: What is greedy decoding?
options: Sampling multiple tokens and selecting the longest response
Always selecting the single highest-probability token at each generation step
Using a large Top-K value to consider many possible next tokens
Running the model multiple times and averaging the outputs
mcqs.explanation: Greedy decoding selects the argmax token (highest probability) at every step. It is deterministic and reproducible, but often produces repetitive or suboptimal text because it never explores lower-probability but potentially better continuations.

mcqs.question: What is the key advantage of Top-P (nucleus) sampling over Top-K sampling?
options: Top-P is always faster to compute than Top-K
Top-P adapts the candidate set size to the distribution shape — small when the model is confident, large when uncertain
Top-P guarantees grammatically correct output
Top-P allows for a deterministic output when P is set to exactly 1.0
mcqs.explanation: Top-K uses a fixed number of candidates regardless of distribution shape. Top-P uses a dynamic candidate set: it includes only the minimal set of tokens whose cumulative probability reaches P. When the model is confident, the nucleus is small (2–3 tokens); when uncertain, it expands automatically.

mcqs.question: For generating production-quality code, which temperature setting is most appropriate?
options: Temperature = 0.0 to 0.2, for deterministic and conservative outputs
Temperature = 0.7, for natural variation in variable names and structure
Temperature = 1.0, to ensure diverse coding approaches
Temperature = 1.5, so the model explores unusual algorithmic solutions
mcqs.explanation: Code generation benefits from low temperature (0–0.2) because correctness is paramount — there are usually right and wrong ways to implement something. Higher temperatures introduce randomness that increases the chance of syntactic or logical errors.

mcqs.question: What is the purpose of a repetition penalty in text generation?
options: To prevent the model from using the same sentence structure twice
To reduce the probability of tokens that have already appeared in the output, discouraging repetitive loops
To ensure the model uses varied vocabulary from a reference corpus
To penalize the model during training when it produces repeated data
mcqs.explanation: Repetition penalty divides the logits of previously generated tokens by a penalty factor (typically 1.1–1.3). This reduces their probability, helping the model avoid generating the same phrases or sentences repeatedly — a common failure mode without this mitigation.

mcqs.question: Why does beam search often produce less natural text than sampling for open-ended generation?
options: Beam search cannot handle context windows longer than 1,024 tokens
Beam search maximizes probability, which tends to favor generic, high-frequency phrases over diverse and natural-sounding continuations
Beam search requires a larger vocabulary than sampling methods
Beam search introduces more randomness than temperature-based sampling
mcqs.explanation: Beam search maintains the B highest-probability sequences, tending to converge on generic phrases that are 'safe' (high probability) but dull. Open-ended generation (chat, stories) benefits from the diversity that sampling introduces, while beam search is still useful for constrained tasks like translation.

flashcards.back: A scalar that controls the randomness of token selection. Logits are divided by T before softmax: T < 1 sharpens the distribution (less random), T > 1 flattens it (more random), T = 0 is greedy (deterministic).

A sampling method that restricts the candidate set to the K highest-probability tokens before sampling. The other tokens have their probabilities zeroed out. Common value: K=50. Limitation: the candidate set size is fixed regardless of distribution confidence.

A sampling method that includes the smallest set of highest-probability tokens whose cumulative probability exceeds P (e.g., P=0.9). Adapts candidate set size to distribution shape. When confident: small nucleus. When uncertain: large nucleus.

The simplest generation strategy: always select the token with the highest probability at each step. Deterministic and reproducible, but tends to produce repetitive and formulaic output. Equivalent to temperature=0.

A function that converts a vector of raw logit scores to a probability distribution (all positive, sum to 1): softmax(xᵢ) = exp(xᵢ) / Σ exp(xⱼ). Applied to LLM logits (after temperature scaling) to produce next-token probabilities.

Probabilistically selecting the next token by drawing from the probability distribution (rather than always taking the argmax). Introduces controlled randomness, producing more natural and diverse text. Governed by temperature, top-K, and top-P.

The raw, unnormalized scores output by the last linear layer of an LLM — one value per vocabulary token. Converted to probabilities via softmax (after temperature scaling). Higher logit = more likely token before softmax.

videos.title: Temperature, Top-P, and Top-K in LLMs explained
videos.description: Clear visual walkthrough of how temperature scaling, top-K filtering, and top-P nucleus sampling affect the token probability distribution, with animated examples showing how each parameter changes the output distribution.

videos.title: Let's build GPT: from scratch, in code — Andrej Karpathy
videos.description: The generation/sampling section of this full GPT build shows exactly how logits are converted to probabilities and sampled at inference time, grounding sampling theory in working code.

practical_description: Interactive sampling playground where you enter a prompt prefix and adjust temperature and top-P sliders in real time, watching the probability distribution bar chart update and seeing how different settings affect which tokens get sampled.

diagram.title: From Logits to a Chosen Word
